It would use technology to support coordination without making attention permanently available. Routine messages would follow a delayed-send protocol, urgent cover would be explicit and teams would agree a response-time norm. Workers would have secure systems, useful flexibility and protected concentration. Managers would measure results while monitoring workload and unequal career effects. Most importantly, people could sign out of work without anxiety that silence signalled weak commitment. Digital tools would remain available, but the organisation—not each individual worker—would carry responsibility for designing humane boundaries. Regular review matters because a reasonable exception can gradually become an ordinary expectation. Regular review matters because a reasonable exception can gradually become an ordinary expectation. Regular review matters because a reasonable exception can gradually become an ordinary expectation.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

A four-day working week should become the standard model for full-time employment. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
occupational recoveryfocus blockcompressed workweekbaseline workload measureworkload redesignpromotion pathwayright to disconnectafter-hours availabilityboundaryless work
Model essay · 315 words

A four-day week promises more rest without a loss of income, and trials have attracted considerable interest. Its advantages can outweigh the disadvantages, but only when organisations remove unnecessary work and protect service quality. Merely compressing the same demands into longer days would replace one working-time problem with another.

The strongest benefit is improved occupational recovery. A regular additional day away from work can support caring, learning and social life, while rested employees may concentrate better during paid hours. The change can also encourage firms to reduce low-value meetings and create a clearer focus block for demanding tasks. These gains may strengthen retention as well as daily productivity. The relevant outcome should be measured over several months rather than inferred from an enthusiastic first week.

However, a compressed workweek can intensify fatigue if staff must complete five days of tasks in four extended shifts. Customer-facing services may need more employees to maintain opening hours, and workers with long commutes or caring duties may dislike very long days. Small firms may also struggle to provide cover unless demand and staffing are analysed carefully. Worker consultation would also reveal whether the apparent benefit is shared across roles and caring arrangements.

For these reasons, implementation should begin with a baseline workload measure and genuine workload redesign. Teams can test fewer meetings, clearer priorities and staggered schedules, then compare output, errors, overtime and wellbeing. Participation should not damage the promotion pathway of anyone who prefers a conventional schedule. A trial is evidence only if it records disadvantages as carefully as positive feedback. Clear responsibility and published evidence would make the policy easier to adjust when unintended effects appear.

Overall, the four-day week is a promising default rather than a universal formula. It offers substantial benefits when fewer days genuinely mean better-designed work. Where it simply concentrates excessive demands, the reform may undermine the recovery it is intended to create.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe employees are mainly responsible for preventing burnout, while others say employers carry the greater responsibility. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
take proper time offstaffing levelindividual coping burdenpsychosocial hazard assessmentrole clarityorganisational-level preventionright to disconnectafter-hours availabilityboundaryless work
Model essay · 305 words

Burnout is often discussed through personal habits such as sleep, exercise and resilience. Individuals do influence how they respond to pressure, but employers carry the greater responsibility because they determine workload, control, support and working-time norms. Personal care is useful only within conditions that make recovery possible.

The argument for individual responsibility is that people differ in boundaries and coping strategies. An employee can request help, take proper time off and avoid unnecessary evening checking. Early action may prevent temporary stress from becoming severe. Employers cannot observe every private difficulty, so workers must communicate when demands or health conditions require adjustment. The relevant outcome should be measured over several months rather than inferred from an enthusiastic first week.

Nevertheless, individuals cannot repair chronic understaffing, bullying or contradictory priorities. When the staffing level is inadequate, advice to relax shifts an individual coping burden onto the person exposed to risk. Employers control job design and should conduct a psychosocial hazard assessment, improve role clarity and ensure that managers can change harmful conditions instead of merely referring employees to an app. Worker consultation would also reveal whether the apparent benefit is shared across roles and caring arrangements.

The most effective approach divides responsibility according to power. Organisations should provide organisational-level prevention, confidential support and a credible right to disconnect. Workers should participate in reviews and use available support, while government sets minimum occupational-health standards. Progress should be assessed through workload, absence, retention and employee accounts rather than a single wellbeing score. Clear responsibility and published evidence would make the policy easier to adjust when unintended effects appear.

In conclusion, workers have a role in recognising strain and seeking assistance, but employers hold greater responsibility for prevention. They create the systems that distribute demands and opportunity. Healthy routines cannot compensate for a workplace whose ordinary operation depends on exhaustion.

TASK 2 · 3

Remote and hybrid work can damage employees’ career progression. What problems does this cause, and what measures can employers take?

Optional collocation bank
proximity biassponsorship gapdigital presenteeismflexibility stigmadevelopmental assignmentcareer conversationpromotion pathwaydistributional workplace effectright to disconnect
Model essay · 301 words

Remote and hybrid work can widen access and reduce commuting, yet they may weaken career progression when opportunity depends on physical visibility. The central problems are unequal information, informal sponsorship and biased evaluation. Employers can preserve flexibility by making development processes more explicit rather than requiring universal office attendance.

One problem is proximity bias. Onsite workers may receive spontaneous advice, desirable projects or early knowledge of a vacancy, while remote colleagues become visible mainly through scheduled output. This can create a sponsorship gap because senior leaders advocate for people they encounter regularly. Remote employees may then compensate through digital presenteeism, extending work into personal time to signal commitment. The relevant outcome should be measured over several months rather than inferred from an enthusiastic first week.

A second problem is that apparently neutral assessment can reward location. Vague criteria such as “leadership presence” may favour office-based behaviour, and managers may overlook collaboration that occurs asynchronously. If carers and disabled workers use flexibility more often, these practices produce wider inequality and a damaging flexibility stigma. Worker consultation would also reveal whether the apparent benefit is shared across roles and caring arrangements.

Employers should publish each developmental assignment, schedule regular career conversation meetings and define a transparent promotion pathway. Hybrid meetings need equal access to information, while performance should be judged through agreed outcomes and professional contribution. Promotion, pay and training data should be examined by work arrangement so that a distributional workplace effect becomes visible and can be investigated. Clear responsibility and published evidence would make the policy easier to adjust when unintended effects appear.

In conclusion, remote work does not inherently block progression; informal and poorly measured career systems do. Transparent opportunities, documented decisions and disaggregated review can protect both flexibility and advancement without forcing employees to trade one for the other.

TASK 2 · 4

People are increasingly likely to change careers several times during their working lives. What causes this trend, and is it a positive or negative development?

Optional collocation bank
career adaptabilityskills portfoliocareer plateaubranch out intocareer capitallateral career movedevelopmental assignmentcareer sustainabilityright to disconnect
Model essay · 300 words

Multiple career changes are becoming more common because technology, longer working lives and shifting personal priorities alter both jobs and workers’ expectations. I regard this as broadly positive when people have access to retraining and financial security, although repeated involuntary transitions can create serious instability.

One cause is structural change. New technologies reduce demand for some tasks while creating unfamiliar roles, so workers need career adaptability and a broader skills portfolio. Longer careers also make it less realistic to choose one occupation in youth and remain satisfied for decades. People may reach a career plateau and seek new learning, purpose or working conditions. The relevant outcome should be measured over several months rather than inferred from an enthusiastic first week.

Career movement can produce valuable benefits. A person who branches out into a related field can transfer knowledge across sectors and build new career capital. Change may also allow workers to leave unhealthy environments or find work that better fits caring responsibilities. Society gains when experience is recombined rather than trapped inside one occupational label. Worker consultation would also reveal whether the apparent benefit is shared across roles and caring arrangements.

However, the positive account assumes choice. Redundancy, insecure contracts and unaffordable training may force workers into lower-paid roles. Governments and employers should support recognised learning, paid training time and reliable career guidance. A gradual lateral career move or developmental assignment can reduce risk by allowing people to test a field before a complete transition. Clear responsibility and published evidence would make the policy easier to adjust when unintended effects appear.

Overall, changing careers can support growth and career sustainability, but only when institutions make transition feasible. The trend is positive when movement expands agency and capability; it is negative when workers repeatedly absorb the cost of economic disruption alone.

TASK 2 · 5

Employers should be required to support mid-career retraining for their staff. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
training budgetinternal mobilitycareer sustainabilitysponsorship gapdevelopmental assignmentright to disconnectafter-hours availabilityboundaryless workdigital presenteeism
Model essay · 309 words

As technology and business models change, workers increasingly need opportunities to update or redirect their skills. I largely agree that employers should support mid-career retraining, although the obligation should be proportionate to organisational size and shared with public systems where training serves a broader transition.

Employers benefit directly from workforce capability. A transparent training budget can help staff develop skills before a shortage becomes acute, while paid learning time makes participation possible for people with caring duties. Retraining may support internal mobility, allowing an organisation to fill new roles without discarding experienced employees whose current tasks are changing. The relevant outcome should be measured over several months rather than inferred from an enthusiastic first week. This safeguard matters because a voluntary opportunity can become a hidden expectation when managers control progression.

There is also a fairness argument. Firms that introduce technology often capture productivity gains, whereas workers face the risk that their expertise loses value. Requiring some support distributes transition costs more evenly and strengthens career sustainability. It may also reduce a sponsorship gap if access to development is advertised rather than negotiated informally. Worker consultation would also reveal whether the apparent benefit is shared across roles and caring arrangements.

Nevertheless, a small employer cannot finance every personal ambition, and highly portable qualifications create benefits beyond one company. Government can provide tax support, individual learning accounts and recognised standards. Employers should consult staff, publish eligibility rules and offer a mixture of short courses, a developmental assignment and formal education. Workers should contribute effort and make reasonable choices among relevant options. Clear responsibility and published evidence would make the policy easier to adjust when unintended effects appear.

In conclusion, mid-career retraining should be a normal employer responsibility, not a discretionary perk. A proportionate shared model would protect workers while recognising that firms, individuals and society all benefit from adaptable skills.
